utilityName: Town of Queen Creek Backflow Program
testingFrequency: Annually after installation and on the BSI filing cycle
dueBasis: Queen Creek requires annual backflow assembly reports through BSI Online, requires current tester licenses and up-to-date gauge calibration certificates, and charges a $14.95 filing fee per test report once the device is in the established reporting cycle.
coveredPropertyTypes: New assemblies entering the Queen Creek system
coveredDeviceTypes: Protected service assemblies entering the town workflow
officialListLabel: Open Queen Creek contractor information for approved tester routing
penalties: Queen Creek says reports will not be accepted if the tester license or test kit calibration is not up to date. Customers stay non-compliant if the accepted BSI workflow is not followed.
sourceExcerpt: Queen Creek is a strong Arizona utility because it turns annual backflow testing into a real BSI workflow with filing fees, contractor approval, and explicit non-acceptance rules for expired credentials.
verdictSummary: Queen Creek is a strong utility because it combines annual backflow reporting, BSI filing fees, and strict tester credential requirements.
whoIsAffected: Queen Creek customers with assemblies that must be reported through the town's BSI-linked backflow workflow, including irrigation and other protected services.
residentialNotes: Residential demand is strongest when the property has an irrigation or other protected service that puts it into the BSI reporting cycle.
The town lets customers use providers not on the list, but only if their credentials are filed and accepted first.
commercialNotes: Commercial value is strong because Queen Creek uses BSI filing fees and formal credential acceptance instead of an ad hoc local process.
This is a clean utility-first page for annual-testing and approved-tester routing.

annualTesting.summary: Queen Creek requires annual test reports through BSI Online and only accepts testers with current licenses and calibration documents.
highlights: The filing fee is $14.95 per test report.
Reports are not accepted if credentials are out of date.
Brand-new assemblies can enter the system without the filing fee in the first year.
workflowSteps: Confirm the assembly is in the Queen Creek BSI cycle.
Use an approved tester or ensure the tester gets credentials accepted first.
File the test through BSI with the correct fee and documentation.

irrigation.summary: Queen Creek is useful for irrigation because the same BSI workflow applies to irrigation-related assemblies and the town openly routes users to approved contractor information.
highlights: Irrigation users still need accepted tester credentials.
Filing fees and accepted BSI reports are the real compliance gate.
workflowSteps: Confirm the irrigation assembly is in the town's reporting system.
Use an approved tester or get the tester approved through BSI.
Submit the result with the accepted fee and credentials.

fireLine.summary: Queen Creek is less public about fire-line nuance than Buckeye, but the same accepted-tester and BSI submission rules still apply to protected services.
highlights: The town directs users to approved contractor information.
Accepted BSI reporting is still the main compliance gate.
workflowSteps: Confirm the protected service and device record.
Submit the result through Queen Creek's accepted workflow.
Check whether the assembly is already in the Queen Creek BSI cycle.
Choose an approved tester or get the tester's credentials accepted.
Submit the accepted report and filing fee so the device does not remain non-compliant.
failureHighlights: Queen Creek has a real filing-fee workflow, not a vague city page.
Expired tester credentials cause report rejection.
Customers can use off-list testers only if those testers become accepted first.

costBand.testingRange: Testing is market-priced, but Queen Creek adds a $14.95 filing fee per report in the regular cycle.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest costs rise if the first vendor cannot get accepted or if the report is rejected for credential issues.
costBand.pricingNotes: The strongest local cost signal is avoiding rejected BSI submissions and repeated test fees.
